1: # MHPCO Claim Office Kata
2: 
3: ## Setting
4: 
5: You are building the policy management system for the **Most Honorable
6: Privileged Claims Office for Magical Risks and Cursed Items** (MHPCO),
7: an old-fashioned insurance institution that covers magical items —
8: swords, amulets, staves, potions, and components such as runes and
9: moonstones. The MHPCO is bureaucratic, slightly stingy, and proud of
10: its venerable traditions.
11: 
12: ## Feature
13: 
14: The system supports two operations: **quote** computes the premium for
15: a list of items a customer wishes to insure, and **claim** processes a
16: damage report against an existing policy.
17: 
18: ## Rules
19: 
20: ### Item values and base premiums
21: 
22: Each main item has an insurance value and a base premium according to
23: the MHPCO price list:
24: 
25: - Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium
26: - Amulet: 600 G / 60 G
27: - Staff: 800 G / 80 G
28: - Potion: 400 G / 40 G
29: 
30: Components — for example runes and moonstones — are insured at 250 G
31: each, with a base premium of 25 G per component. A building block of
32: 3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G.
33: 
34: ### Premium modifiers
35: 
36: - Cursed items add a 50 % risk surcharge.
37: - Highly enchanted items (enchantment level ≥ 5) add a 30 % risk
38:   surcharge.
39: - Long-standing customers (≥ 2 years of business with MHPCO) receive a
40:   20 % loyalty discount.
41: - A first insurance carries a 10 % initial assessment surcharge.
42: - Customers receive a 15 % discount on each contract after their first.
43: - A 5 G processing fee is added to every premium.
44: 
45: All amounts are rounded to whole G in the MHPCO's favor.
46: 
47: ### Claim processing
48: 
49: A deductible of 100 G applies per damage event. The total payout per
50: policy is capped at twice the insurance sum.
51: 
52: - Damage to items with enchantment level ≥ 8 is reimbursed at 50 % of
53:   the damage amount.
54: - Damage to items made of dragon material is fully reimbursed.
55: 
56: ## Examples
57: 
58: The examples below illustrate single rules in isolation. Values shown
59: are base premiums or raw payout amounts before integration with other
60: modifiers, unless stated otherwise.
61: 
62: ### Building block of 3 alike components
63: 
64: - 2 runes → 50 G base premium
65: - 3 runes → 60 G base premium (block applies)
66: - 4 runes → 100 G base premium (no block — block requires exactly 3)
67: - 7 runes → 175 G base premium
68: 
69: ### "Alike" components
70: 
71: > ❓ Hmm — "alike" components: does that mean exactly the same type,
72: > or just the same family (rune-y, gemstone-y)?
73: 
74: - 2 runes + 1 moonstone → 75 G base premium (no block: different types)
75: - 3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G base premium (two separate blocks)
76: 
77: ### Modifier scope on multi-item policies
78: 
79: > ❓ A policy with several items: does the cursed surcharge apply to
80: > the whole policy, or just to the cursed item?
81: 
82: - a policy covers a cursed sword (base premium 100 G) and a plain
83:   amulet (base premium 60 G) → policy base premium 160 G; the cursed
84:   surcharge adds 50 G (50 % of the cursed sword's base premium, not
85:   of the policy total) → 210 G before further modifiers and fee
86: - item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the
87:   base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty,
88:   first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base
89:   premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is
90:   added at the very end
91: 
92: ### Modifier thresholds
93: 
94: - customer with exactly 2 years with MHPCO → loyalty discount applies
95: - sword with exactly enchantment 5 → high-enchantment surcharge
96:   applies; if cursed, both surcharges apply
97: - sword with enchantment 4 → no high-enchantment surcharge; curse
98:   surcharge applies only if cursed
99: - dragon-material sword with exactly enchantment 8, damage 1000 G →
100:   payout 400 G (high-enchantment clause applies, then deductible)
101: 
102: ### Deductible per damage event
103: 
104: - a dragon attack damages an insured sword (500 G) and an insured
105:   amulet (300 G); payout = 600 G (the 100 G deductible applies once
106:   per damaged item)
107: 
108: ### Standard reimbursement (no special clauses)
109: 
110: - regular sword (steel, enchantment 3), damage 500 G → payout 400 G
111:   (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; no special clause applies)
112: - damage to a rune (insurance value 250 G), damage 200 G → payout
113:   100 G (full reimbursement minus 100 G deductible; runes have no
114:   enchantment level or material, so no special clause applies)
115: 
116: ### Enchantment threshold vs. dragon material
117: 
118: - dragon-material sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
119:   (both clauses apply; the 50 % rule wins, then deductible: 500 − 100)
120: - dragon-material sword, enchantment 5, damage 800 G → payout 700 G
121:   (only the dragon-material clause applies: full reimbursement,
122:   then deductible: 800 − 100)
123: - steel sword, enchantment 9, damage 1000 G → payout 400 G
124:   (only the high-enchantment clause applies: 50 % first, then
125:   deductible: 500 − 100)
126: 
127: ### Multiple items of the same type
128: 
129: > ❓ What if a customer brings two of the same item — one in hand,
130: > one in the backpack? Or just two swords because they fight with
131: > two?
132: 
133: - a policy covers two swords → insurance sum 2000 G (= 2×1000),
134:   cap 4000 G
135: - a dragon attack damages both swords; `damages` contains two
136:   `{itemType: "sword", ...}` entries → each entry is treated as a
137:   separate damage with its own deductible
138: - if the `damages` array contains more entries of a given type than
139:   the policy actually covers (e.g. two sword damages but only one
140:   sword insured) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code; the
141:   whole claim is rejected
142: 
143: ### Cap exhaustion
144: 
145: - a policy covers a sword and an amulet → insurance sum 1600 G
146:   (= 1000 + 600, the sum of the items' insurance values), cap 3200 G
147: - a cursed sword (insurance value 1000 G, premium with modifiers
148:   165 G) → cap 2000 G (based on the unmodified insurance value;
149:   premium modifiers do not raise the cap)
150: - a policy covers a sword and 3 runes (a block) → insurance sum
151:   1750 G (= 1000 + 3×250); the block discount affects the premium
152:   only, not the insurance sum
153: - a sword is insured (insurance sum 1000 G, cap 2000 G); two successive
154:   claims of 1500 G each
155: - first claim → payout 1400 G, cap remaining 600 G
156: - second claim → payout 600 G, cap remaining 0 G (the desired 1400 G
157:   is reduced to the remaining cap)
158: 
159: ### Rounding in the MHPCO's favor
160: 
161: - a premium calculation that yields 197.5 G → final premium 198 G
162:   (rounded up)
163: - a payout calculation that yields 350.5 G → final payout 350 G
164:   (rounded down)
165: - intermediate amounts during a calculation are kept as fractions;
166:   only the final premium or payout is rounded
167: 
168: ### Edge cases
169: 
170: - empty item list → premium 5 G (only the processing fee)
171: - quote includes an item with an unknown type (e.g.
172:   `{type: "broomstick"}`) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
173:   and writes an error description to stderr; no `results` are written
174:   to stdout
175: - claim references a damage entry whose item is not part of the policy
176:   (e.g. an amulet damaged when only a sword is insured, or an item
177:   with an unknown type) → the CLI exits with a non-zero status code
178:   and writes an error description to stderr
179: - claim contains a damage entry with `amount: -200` → the CLI exits
180:   with a non-zero status code and writes an error description to
181:   stderr
182: 
183: ## Integration examples
184: 
185: The following two examples combine multiple rules to clarify how
186: modifiers stack and how customer history affects the premium.
187: 
188: ### Newcomer with a cursed sword
189: 
190: - customer: 0 years with MHPCO, no previous contract
191: - item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 3)
192: - premium: 165 G
193: - (100 G base + 50 G curse + 10 G first insurance = 160 G + 5 G fee = 165 G)
194: 
195: ### Long-standing customer's second contract
196: 
197: > ❓ Wait — does "first insurance" mean the customer's first ever
198: > contract, or the first time we see this particular item? A
199: > long-standing customer with a brand-new sword: does the first
200: > insurance surcharge still apply?
201: 
202: - customer: 3 years with MHPCO; this is the customer's second `quote`
203:   in the scenario
204: - item: a cursed sword (steel, enchantment 7)
205: - premium: 160 G
206: - (100 G base + 50 G curse + 30 G high enchantment − 20 G loyalty
207:   + 10 G first insurance − 15 G follow-up contract = 155 G + 5 G fee
208:   = 160 G)
209: - The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even
210:   though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a
211:   `quote` is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer
212:   history.
213: 
214: ## CLI input/output format
215: 
216: The implementation must be exposed as a command-line executable
217: `claim-office`. It reads a JSON document from stdin describing a
218: scenario and writes a JSON document with the corresponding results to
219: stdout.
220: 
221: A scenario is a JSON object with a `customer` object and a `steps`
222: array. The `customer` describes the single customer for the whole
223: scenario. Each step has an `op` field (`"quote"` or `"claim"`) and the
224: operation-specific input fields. Steps are processed sequentially;
225: later `claim` steps refer to a policy created by an earlier `quote`
226: step via its zero-based step index in a `policy` field.
227: 
228: Stdout receives a JSON object with a `results` array of the same length
229: and order as the input `steps`. Each result contains:
230: 
231: - For a `quote` step: `premium` (integer, total premium in G).
232: - For a `claim` step: `payout` (integer, payout in G) and
233:   `remainingCap` (integer, cap remaining on the policy after this
234:   claim).
235: 
236: ### JSON Schema (normative)
237: 
238: The field names below are **binding**. Your implementation must use
239: exactly these names; do not rename or restructure them.
240: 
241: **Input (stdin):**
242: 
243: ```json
244: {
245:   "type": "object",
246:   "required": ["customer", "steps"],
247:   "properties": {
248:     "customer": {
249:       "type": "object",
250:       "required": ["yearsWithMHPCO"],
251:       "properties": {
252:         "yearsWithMHPCO": { "type": "integer" }
253:       }
254:     },
255:     "steps": {
256:       "type": "array",
257:       "items": {
258:         "oneOf": [
259:           {
260:             "description": "Quote step",
261:             "required": ["op", "items"],
262:             "properties": {
263:               "op": { "const": "quote" },
264:               "items": {
265:                 "type": "array",
266:                 "items": {
267:                   "type": "object",
268:                   "required": ["type"],
269:                   "properties": {
270:                     "type":        { "type": "string", "description": "sword | amulet | staff | potion | rune | moonstone" },
271:                     "material":    { "type": "string" },
272:                     "enchantment": { "type": "integer" },
273:                     "cursed":      { "type": "boolean" }
274:                   }
275:                 }
276:               }
277:             }
278:           },
279:           {
280:             "description": "Claim step",
281:             "required": ["op", "policy", "incident"],
282:             "properties": {
283:               "op":     { "const": "claim" },
284:               "policy": { "type": "integer", "description": "zero-based index of the quote step that created the policy" },
285:               "incident": {
286:                 "type": "object",
287:                 "required": ["cause", "damages"],
288:                 "properties": {
289:                   "cause": { "type": "string" },
290:                   "damages": {
291:                     "type": "array",
292:                     "items": {
293:                       "type": "object",
294:                       "required": ["itemType", "amount"],
295:                       "properties": {
296:                         "itemType": { "type": "string" },
297:                         "amount":   { "type": "integer" }
298:                       }
299:                     }
300:                   }
301:                 }
302:               }
303:             }
304:           }
305:         ]
306:       }
307:     }
308:   }
309: }
310: ```
311: 
312: **Output (stdout):**
313: 
314: ```json
315: {
316:   "type": "object",
317:   "required": ["results"],
318:   "properties": {
319:     "results": {
320:       "type": "array",
321:       "items": {
322:         "oneOf": [
323:           {
324:             "description": "Quote result",
325:             "required": ["premium"],
326:             "properties": {
327:               "premium": { "type": "integer" }
328:             }
329:           },
330:           {
331:             "description": "Claim result",
332:             "required": ["payout", "remainingCap"],
333:             "properties": {
334:               "payout":       { "type": "integer" },
335:               "remainingCap": { "type": "integer" }
336:             }
337:           }
338:         ]
339:       }
340:     }
341:   }
342: }
343: ```
344: 
345: ### Schema example
346: 
347: Stdin:
348: 
349: ```json
350: {
351:   "customer": {"yearsWithMHPCO": 5},
352:   "steps": [
353:     {
354:       "op": "quote",
355:       "items": [
356:         {"type": "amulet", "material": "silver", "enchantment": 2, "cursed": false}
357:       ]
358:     },
359:     {
360:       "op": "claim",
361:       "policy": 0,
362:       "incident": {
363:         "cause": "fire",
364:         "damages": [
365:           {"itemType": "amulet", "amount": 200}
366:         ]
367:       }
368:     }
369:   ]
370: }
371: ```
372: 
373: Stdout shape:
374: 
375: ```json
376: {"results": [{"premium": <integer>}, {"payout": <integer>, "remainingCap": <integer>}]}
377: ```
378: 
379: ## Expected Output Files
380: 
381: - Source files implementing the logic and the `claim-office` CLI
382: - Test files
383: - CLI entry point at `src/cli.ts` (reads JSON from stdin, writes JSON to stdout)
384: 
385: ## Constraints
386: 
387: - Use TypeScript for the implementation
388: - The CLI must read JSON from stdin and write JSON to stdout
389: - The CLI entry point must be at `src/cli.ts`
390: - Internal data structures and module organization are free

(End of file - total 390 lines)
</content>

<skill_content name="test-list">
# Skill: test-list

# TDD Test List Phase

You are now in the **Test List Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to create a comprehensive test list.

## Your Mission

Create a test list using `it.todo()` that covers **every rule and every example** from the specification:
1. Read the specification (`prompt.md`) thoroughly -- every rule, every example, every clarifying question (?)
2. Turn each example into at least one `it.todo()` test case
3. Order tests from simplest to most complex
4. Use `it.todo()` only -- NO executable tests yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Process

### Step 1: Understand the Feature
Read the complete specification. Pay special attention to integration examples and clarifying
questions (marked with ?) -- these disambiguate rules that may seem open to interpretation in isolation.
- What are all the operations the system must support?
- What rules govern each operation?
- Which examples in the spec illustrate these rules?

### Step 2: Identify Test Cases from the Spec
Walk through the specification section by section. For each rule and each example:
- Create a test case that verifies the described behavior
- Include the **expected values from the spec** in the test description
- If a clarifying question (?) resolves an ambiguity, create a test for the clarified interpretation
- If the spec uses an example-mapping format (rules, examples, questions), every listed example must have a corresponding test

### Step 3: Order Tests (Simple -> Complex)
Arrange tests in increasing complexity:
1. Simplest case (often empty/zero/single item)
2. Individual rules in isolation
3. Rules with modifiers
4. Combinations of multiple rules
5. Multi-step scenarios (e.g., operations that reference earlier results)

### Step 4: Write Test File
Create the test file with `it.todo()` entries:

```typescript
import { describe, it, expect } from "vitest";
import { functionName } from "./implementation.js";

describe("Feature Name", () => {
  it.todo("should [behavior] -- [expected value from spec]");
  it.todo("should [next behavior] -- [expected value from spec]");
  // ... ordered simple -> complex, covering ALL spec examples
});
```

### Step 5: Provide Summary

After creating the test list, output:

```
Test List Created:
**Feature**: [feature name]
**Test File**: [filename].spec.ts
**Tests**: [count]

**Test Cases** (ordered simple -> complex):
1. [first test description]
2. [second test description]
3. [third test description]
...

**Next Step**: Invoke `red` skill to activate the first test.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Cover **every spec example** with at least one test
- Cover **every operation** described in the spec
- Give **every clarifying question (?)** a corresponding test
- Order tests **simple -> complex**
- Use `it.todo()` for all tests
- Include **expected values** in descriptions
- Keep tests **independent**
- One behavior per test

### DON'T
- Write executable tests (use `it.todo()`)
- Think about implementation instead of behavior
- Miss an entire operation described in the spec
- Order randomly

## Completion

After completing the test list, proceed to Red phase:

```
Test List Phase Complete. Proceeding to Red phase with the first test.
```

# Skill: red

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. Maintain strict discipline - NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Compilation error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number; // Intentionally wrong
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Assertion error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

**Why this format matters:** The block is mechanically parsed to compute
`predictions_correct_rate`. The parser expects two lines matching
`(- |Correct|Incorrect)` per cycle -- one for the compilation prediction,
one for the runtime prediction. Collapsing them into a single line, summarizing
them as "both correct", or skipping the block entirely drops the predictions
count for this cycle to zero. Format consistency here directly drives a metric
the experiment measures.

```
Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- Make explicit predictions before running tests
- Verify test fails for the right reason
- Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- Activate multiple tests
- Skip making predictions
- Write implementation to make test pass
- Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

<skill_content name="green">
# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers" - NOW generalize
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Write minimal code to make test pass
- Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- Take baby steps
- Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- Implement beyond what tests demand
- Add features for future tests
- Optimize prematurely
- Refactor during Green phase

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

Trust the process. Simple steps compound into elegant solutions.

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers" - NOW generalize
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Write minimal code to make test pass
- Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- Take baby steps
- Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- Implement beyond what tests demand
- Add features for future tests
- Optimize prematurely
- Refactor during Green phase

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

Trust the process. Simple steps compound into elegant solutions.

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

# Skill: red

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. Maintain strict discipline - NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Compilation error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number; // Intentionally wrong
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Assertion error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

**Why this format matters:** The block is mechanically parsed to compute
`predictions_correct_rate`. The parser expects two lines matching
`(- |Correct|Incorrect)` per cycle -- one for the compilation prediction,
one for the runtime prediction. Collapsing them into a single line, summarizing
them as "both correct", or skipping the block entirely drops the predictions
count for this cycle to zero. Format consistency here directly drives a metric
the experiment measures.

```
Red Phase Complete:
**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- Make explicit predictions before running tests
- Verify test fails for the right reason
- Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- Activate multiple tests
- Skip making predictions
- Write implementation to make test pass
- Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.
```

<skill_content name="green">
# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers" - NOW generalize
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Write minimal code to make test pass
- Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- Take baby steps
- Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- Implement beyond what tests demand
- Add features for future tests
- Optimize prematurely
- Refactor during Green phase

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

Trust the process. Simple steps compound into elegant solutions.

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

Clean compile, all tests green.

## Step 5: Calculate New APP Mass

The change replaces `Record<string, number>` (type annotation, doesn't contribute to runtime mass) with `as const`, and adds a `KnownItemType` type alias (also type-only). The runtime mass of `BASE_PRICES` is unchanged. `baseOf` adds a type-cast `as KnownItemType` which is type-only (zero runtime cost). 

**New Total Mass ≈ 64** (unchanged at runtime — type-only changes)

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied: Tighten `BASE_PRICES` typing with `as const`**

Before:
```typescript
const BASE_PRICES: Record<string, number> = { ... };
const baseOf = (item: Item): number => BASE_PRICES[item.type];
```

After:
```typescript
const BASE_PRICES = { sword: 100, ... } as const;
type KnownItemType = keyof typeof BASE_PRICES;
const baseOf = (item: Item): number => BASE_PRICES[item.type as KnownItemType];
```

**Benefits (Rule 2 — Reveals Intent):**
- The set of *known* item types is now visible in the type system (`KnownItemType` is derived from the constants)
- `Record<string, number>` was lying — it claimed any string had a price; `as const` tells the truth: only these five strings have prices
- Sets up cleaner ground for the upcoming `"unknown item type in quote exits non-zero"` test, which can now check membership against `BASE_PRICES` without a separate registry

**APP Mass:** Unchanged at runtime (~64). The new `KnownItemType` and `as const` are type-only constructs with no execution cost.

**Trade-off:** Added an explicit type-cast in `baseOf`. This is a small cost paid for type honesty — the cast makes explicit the (current) assumption that all items have known types, which is exactly the assumption an upcoming test will challenge.

## Step 7: Report

**Refactoring Complete**
- **Refactoring**: Tightened `BASE_PRICES` to a literal-typed `as const` object and introduced `KnownItemType` derived from its keys. Replaces the loose `Record<string, number>` annotation that allowed any string lookup.
- **Mass Change**: ~64 → ~64 (type-only refactor; no runtime mass change, but type-system intent improved)
- **Naming Evaluation**: All function and constant names (`runScenario`, `runStep`, `premiumFor`, `sumBases`, `baseOf`, `BASE_PRICES`, `PROCESSING_FEE`) accurately reveal intent — no renames needed
- **Tests**: All 6 passing, 32 todo, TypeScript compiles cleanly
</task_result>

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

};

- This will scale better as more block/discount rules are added (the test list shows `7 runes → 175 G` and `3 runes + 3 moonstones → 120 G with two separate blocks` coming next).

**Other Refactorings Considered, Not Applied**:
- **Extract `baseOf` inline**: Used only once in the reduce; could inline. Rejected — the helper isolates the `as KnownItemType` cast and reads more clearly than inlining.
- **Simplify `isRuneBlock`**: Already minimal — length check short-circuits before `every`.
- **Combine `premiumFor` and `baseTotal`**: Would couple base-calculation with fee logic. Rejected — separation matches upcoming modifiers (cursed surcharge applies to base, fee is added after).
- **Replace `runStep` throw with a real claim implementation**: Out of scope; no test demands it yet (Rule 4: fewest elements until forced).

## Step 7: Report

**Refactoring Complete**:
- **Refactoring**: Renamed `sumBases` → `baseTotal` (Rule 2 — better reveals intent; the function returns a discounted block price, not a literal sum)
- **Mass Change**: 87 → 87 (no element change; pure clarity improvement — Rule 2 trumps APP)
- **Tests**: All 8 passing, 30 todo

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript

return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers" - NOW generalize
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Write minimal code to make test pass
- Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- Take baby steps
- Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- Implement beyond what tests demand
- Add features for future tests
- Optimize prematurely
- Refactor during Green phase

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

Trust the process. Simple steps compound into elegant solutions.

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## APP Mass — After

```ts
const sumOfBases = (items: Item[]): number =>

- Constants: 1 (0) = 1
- Bindings: 3 (items, total, item) = 3

- Loops: 1 = 5

```ts
const baseTotal = (items: Item[]): number =>
  isRuneBlock(items) ? RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE : sumOfBases(items);
```
- Bindings: 2 (items refs, RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE) = 2
- Invocations: 2 (isRuneBlock, sumOfBases) = 4
- Conditionals: 1 (ternary) = 4
- **Mass: 10**

Combined: 25 (vs 22 before) — slightly higher by 3 due to extra invocation/binding from extraction.

## Final Report

**Refactoring Applied**: Extracted `sumOfBases` from `baseTotal` and rewrote `baseTotal` as a single declarative ternary expression.

**Before**:
```ts
const baseTotal = (items: Item[]): number => {
  if (isRuneBlock(items)) return RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE;
  return items.reduce((total, item) => total + baseOf(item), 0);
};
```

**After**:
```ts
const sumOfBases = (items: Item[]): number =>
  items.reduce((total, item) => total + baseOf(item), 0);

const baseTotal = (items: Item[]): number =>
  isRuneBlock(items) ? RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE : sumOfBases(items);
```

**Mass Change**: 22 → 25 (delta +3) for the affected functions.

**Why accept the slight mass increase?** Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) trumps APP. The new shape makes the pricing rule read as a single sentence: *"base total is the rune-block price if it's a rune block, otherwise the sum of item bases."* Each helper now has one clear job:
- `sumOfBases` — pure summing of item base prices
- `baseTotal` — selects between block pricing and plain summing
- `isRuneBlock` — predicate

This will also pay off for upcoming tests like "3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks", where the selection logic in `baseTotal` will naturally evolve (e.g., partition into blocks then sum), while `sumOfBases` remains the unchanged plain-sum primitive.

**Naming Evaluation**: All function names still fit. No renames performed — `baseTotal`, `sumOfBases`, `isRuneBlock`, `premiumFor`, `baseOf`, `runStep`, `runScenario` each clearly reveal their purpose given the current 9 passing tests.

**Tests**: All 9 passing, 29 todo. Green maintained.

## Refactoring Report

### Naming Evaluation (First Priority)
- `baseOf` → `basePriceOf`: "base of what?" was ambiguous. The function returns a base **price**, so the explicit name reveals intent better. Domain involves multiple "base" things (base premium, base price), so disambiguating helps.
- `sumOfBases` → `sumOfBasePrices`: Same reasoning, consistent with `basePriceOf`.
- `isRuneBlock`, `baseTotal`, `premiumFor`, `runStep`, `runScenario`: All reveal intent well — kept as-is.
- The recent Green change (adding `moonstone: 25`) did NOT change the nature of `isRuneBlock` — it remains correctly scoped to "3 runes" specifically, and the failing-then-passing test for "2 runes + 1 moonstone" confirmed the predicate excludes mixed types. Name still fits.

### APP Mass (approximate)
- **Before**: ~69 units
- **After**: ~69 units (rename doesn't change structural mass — same bindings, invocations, etc.)
- **Mass change**: 0 (renames don't affect APP)

### Rules Applied
- **Rule 1 (Tests Pass)**: ✓ All 10 tests still green after the rename.
- **Rule 2 (Reveals Intent)**: ✓ Primary improvement — `basePriceOf` and `sumOfBasePrices` are more specific than `baseOf`/`sumOfBases` and avoid ambiguity in a domain where "base" can mean base premium, base price, base cap, etc.
- **Rule 3 (No Duplication)**: No new duplication; `rune: 25` and `moonstone: 25` sharing a value is coincidence at this point (no test groups them).
- **Rule 4 (Fewest Elements)**: Considered inlining `RUNE_BLOCK_SIZE` and `RUNE_BLOCK_PRICE` since they're each used once, but the named constants serve Rule 2 (the spec literally says "blocks of 3 runes form a discount") — kept for intent. Considered simplifying `runStep`'s conditional, but TypeScript's discriminated union requires the narrowing, so the conditional carries necessary type information.

### Why no structural refactor?
- `baseTotal`'s `if-block ? block-price : sum` is a direct expression of the spec rule and only 1 conditional + 1 ternary — already minimal.
- `runStep`'s throw branch is anticipation of claim tests still in `it.todo()`. It could be argued as speculative (Rule 4), but removing the `ClaimStep` from the discriminated union would (a) require changing exported types used by the spec's future tests and (b) make the type system unable to express the claim path that's clearly in the spec. The current shape is the minimum that keeps the type discriminated.
- The 10 passing tests don't justify pulling block-discount into a more general "block detector" — that would be speculation about the moonstone block test still pending.

### Result
**Refactoring**: Renamed `baseOf` → `basePriceOf` and `sumOfBases` → `sumOfBasePrices` for clearer intent (Rule 2).
**Mass Change**: ~69 → ~69 (rename only; structurally identical)
**Tests**: 10 passing, 28 todo — all green.

# Skill: red

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list
2. Make explicit predictions about how it will fail
3. Verify the test fails for the right reason
4. Maintain strict discipline - NO implementation during Red phase

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Compilation Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Compilation error
- Reason: Function `calculate` doesn't exist yet
- Error: "Cannot find name 'calculate'"
```

### Step 3: Run Test - Verify Compilation Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Compilation error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 4: Create Empty Function

Create minimal function stub (no logic):

```typescript
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return undefined as unknown as number; // Intentionally wrong
};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

Before running again, state your prediction:

```
Red Phase - Runtime Error Prediction:
- Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
- Expected: Runtime assertion error
- Expected value: 0
- Actual value: undefined
- Diff:
  Expected: 0
  Received: undefined
```

### Step 6: Run Test - Verify Runtime Error

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Assertion error as predicted, OR
- Prediction wrong -> STOP and explain discrepancy

### Step 7: Report Completion

You MUST output the full Step 7 block verbatim with `Correct` or `Incorrect`
chosen for each prediction. Do not abbreviate. Do not collapse the two
prediction lines into one.

**Why this format matters:** The block is mechanically parsed to compute
`predictions_correct_rate`. The parser expects two lines matching
`(- |Correct|Incorrect)` per cycle -- one for the compilation prediction,
one for the runtime prediction. Collapsing them into a single line, summarizing
them as "both correct", or skipping the block entirely drops the predictions
count for this cycle to zero. Format consistency here directly drives a metric
the experiment measures.

```

**Test Activated**: "should return 0 for empty input"
**Compilation Prediction**: Cannot find name 'calculate' Correct
**Runtime Prediction**: Expected 0, received undefined Correct
**Result**: Test fails as expected with assertion error

Proceeding to Green phase.
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Activate exactly ONE test at a time
- Make explicit predictions before running tests
- Verify test fails for the right reason
- Keep all other tests as `it.todo()`

### DON'T
- Activate multiple tests
- Skip making predictions
- Write implementation to make test pass
- Continue if prediction fails without explanation

## Prediction Failure Protocol

If your prediction was wrong:

```
Prediction Failed:
- Predicted: [what you expected]
- Actual: [what happened]
- Discrepancy: [explanation]

Investigating the discrepancy before proceeding.
```

## Completion

After completing Red phase, proceed to Green phase:

```
Red Phase Complete. Proceeding to Green phase.

<skill_content name="green">
# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers" - NOW generalize
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Write minimal code to make test pass
- Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- Take baby steps
- Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- Implement beyond what tests demand
- Add features for future tests
- Optimize prematurely
- Refactor during Green phase

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

Trust the process. Simple steps compound into elegant solutions.

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

parts.text:
Now I need a generalized block detection. The block applies per component type: group items by type, and if a component type has exactly 3, charge BLOCK_PRICE instead of 3×base. Non-component items (sword, etc.) never form blocks.

Looking at the spec carefully: "A building block of 3 alike components is offered at a special base premium of 60 G." Components are runes, moonstones, etc. Components have base price 25 each. A block applies when there are exactly 3 of the SAME component type.

So the general approach: partition items by type. For each group:
- if group has 3 components → 60 G
- otherwise → count × basePrice

Let me look at the test list — there's no test yet for "6 runes" alone. With 3 runes I get 60; with 4 runes I get 100 (no block, plain summing); with 6 runes, what happens? Spec doesn't explicitly say. But "block requires exactly 3" — so 6 runes would NOT be 2 blocks (because spec example "4 runes → no block" suggests strict equality).

But wait — the test "3 runes + 3 moonstones → two separate blocks" means each type group of 3 forms its own block. So my logic: per-type groups of exactly 3 components → 60 each; otherwise sum normally.

Minimal implementation: group by type, then for each component-type group with count === 3 use 60, else sum normally.

**Net change**: removed 1 loop + 2 assignments (17 mass) from `baseTotal`, added a small extracted helper `isComponentBlock` (9 mass). Net reduction: ~8 mass.

More importantly, the **clarity (Rule 2) improved**:
- `isComponentBlock(group)` reads as a named predicate revealing intent
- `baseTotal` is now a pure functional one-liner: "sum the base totals of all groups"
- `baseTotalForGroup` no longer needs the redundant `type` parameter (the type is already implicit in the group)

## Step 6: Document Decision

**Refactoring Applied**:

1. **Extracted `isComponentBlock(group)` predicate** (Rule 2 - Reveals Intent): The condition `COMPONENT_TYPES.has(type) && group.length === COMPONENT_BLOCK_SIZE` now has a name that describes what it means in domain terms.

2. **Removed redundant `type` parameter from `baseTotalForGroup`** (Rule 4 - Fewest Elements): A group's type is implicitly `group[0].type`, so passing it separately was duplication of state. Signature dropped from `(type, group)` to `(group)`.

3. **Converted `baseTotal` from imperative loop+accumulator to functional `reduce`** (APP mass reduction): Eliminated 1 mutable `let` + 1 loop + 2 assignments. `baseTotal` is now a single expression body.

4. **Changed `groupByType` return type from `Map<string, Item[]>` to `Item[][]`** (Rule 4 + Rule 2): Downstream code only needed the groups (values), not the keys. Returning the simpler structure is honest about what callers actually use.

**Mass Change**: ~63 → ~55 (≈ -8). The biggest win was removing the two assignments (cost 6 each = 12 mass) in `baseTotal`.

**Tests**: All 11 passing, 27 todo (unchanged).

---

## Report

**Refactoring**: 
- Extracted `isComponentBlock` predicate to name a domain concept
- Removed redundant `type` parameter from `baseTotalForGroup` (group already knows its type)
- Rewrote `baseTotal` as a functional reduce, eliminating a mutable accumulator and `for` loop
- Simplified `groupByType` return type to `Item[][]` (callers don't need the keys)

**Mass Change**: ~63 → ~55 (eliminated 1 loop + 2 assignments; added small extracted predicate)

**Tests**: All 11 passing.

# TDD Red Phase

You are now in the **Red Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to activate ONE test and make it fail.

## Your Mission

1. Activate exactly ONE test from the test list

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Red Phase Rules

- **One test at a time**: Convert exactly ONE `it.todo()` to executable test
- **All other tests remain as `it.todo()`**: Never have more than one failing test
- **Two-stage failure**: First compilation error, then runtime/assertion error
- **Make predictions**: Explicitly state expected failures before running tests
- **No implementation**: Don't write code to make test pass yet

## Process

### Step 1: Activate One Test

Identify the next `it.todo()` and convert it to executable test code:

```typescript
// Convert from:
it.todo("should return 0 for empty input");

// To:
it("should return 0 for empty input", () => {
  expect(calculate("")).toBe(0);
});
```

Leave all other tests as `it.todo()`.

### Step 2: Predict Compilation Error

Before running the test, state your prediction:

};
```

### Step 5: Predict Runtime Error

<skill_content name="green">
# Skill: green

# TDD Green Phase

You are now in the **Green Phase** of TDD. Follow these instructions to make the failing test pass with MINIMAL code.

## Why minimality matters

The Green Phase deliberately writes the smallest implementation that turns the active test green -- even hardcoded returns, even obviously-incomplete logic. This is not laziness or naivete:

- It **exposes refactoring opportunities** in the next phase. When the implementation does exactly one thing, structural smells are visible. Over-implemented green code looks "already clean" and hides design problems.
- It **prevents premature generalization**. The shape of the right abstraction emerges from the second and third test, not the first. Implementing for hypothetical future tests locks in a design before its constraints are known.
- It **keeps the red-green-refactor cycle short**. Each cycle pays a cost in tokens and context; over-implementing in green collapses several future cycles into one large one and loses the per-test feedback loop.

## Your Mission

1. Implement the **minimal code** necessary to make the failing test pass
2. Use the **simplest possible solution** (hardcoded values are acceptable)
3. Avoid adding features for future tests
4. Verify all tests pass
5. NO optimization, NO refactoring yet

## Context: $ARGUMENTS

## Green Phase Rules

- **Minimal code only**: Just enough to pass the current test
- **Baby steps**: Make the smallest possible change
- **No future features**: Don't implement what future tests might need
- **Simple is better**: Hardcoded returns are perfectly fine
- **Tests must pass**: Verify all tests are green
- **No refactoring yet**: Save improvements for Refactor phase

## Process

### Step 1: Analyze the Failing Test

Understand what the test expects:
- What input does the test provide?
- What output does it expect?
- What is the **simplest** way to produce that output?

### Step 2: Write Minimal Implementation

Implement **only what's needed** to make the current test pass:

```typescript
// For first test "should return 0 for empty input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  return 0; // Minimal - just make the test pass
};

// For second test "should return number for single input":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  return parseInt(input); // Still simple
};

// For third test "should add two numbers":
export const calculate = (input: string): number => {
  if (input === "") return 0;
  const numbers = input.split(",");
  if (numbers.length === 1) return parseInt(numbers[0]);
  return parseInt(numbers[0]) + parseInt(numbers[1]); // Only now add logic
};
```

### Step 3: Run Tests

Run `pnpm test` and verify:
- Current test now passes
- All previous tests still pass

### Step 4: Verify No Over-Implementation

Check yourself:
- Did I implement features for future tests? -> Remove them
- Did I add logic not demanded by current test? -> Remove it
- Did I optimize prematurely? -> Simplify
- Did I refactor existing code? -> Revert, save for Refactor phase

### Step 5: Report Completion

```
Green Phase Complete:
**Implementation**: [brief description of what was added]
**Result**: All tests now pass ([X] passing)
**Approach**: [explain why this is minimal]

Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

## Minimal Implementation Strategies

### Hardcoded Returns (Preferred for Early Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return 0 for empty input"
return 0; // Perfect - minimal
```

### Simple Conditionals (When Multiple Tests)
```typescript
// Test: "should return number for single input"
if (input === "") return 0;
return parseInt(input); // Still simple
```

### Generalization (Only When Forced)
```typescript
// Test: "should add multiple numbers" - NOW generalize
return input.split(",").reduce((sum, n) => sum + parseInt(n), 0);
```

## Important Guidelines

### DO
- Write minimal code to make test pass
- Use hardcoded values when appropriate
- Take baby steps
- Verify all tests pass

### DON'T
- Implement beyond what tests demand
- Add features for future tests
- Optimize prematurely
- Refactor during Green phase

## Psychological Resistance

You will feel resistance:
- **"This is too simple"** -> That's correct! Minimal is the way
- **"Hardcoded values feel wrong"** -> They're exactly right for early tests
- **"I should implement ahead"** -> Resist this strongly
- **"This is inefficient"** -> Actually accelerates development

Trust the process. Simple steps compound into elegant solutions.

## Completion

After completing Green phase, proceed to Refactor phase:

```
Green Phase Complete. Proceeding to Refactor phase.
```

So "first insurance" means... the FIRST CONTRACT for that customer. The newcomer's first quote = first contract → first insurance surcharge.

But then "each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance" from the integration example for a NEWCOMER having only one item — that just says the surcharge is per-item, not per-quote.

The long-standing customer integration: 2nd contract, gets +10 G first insurance. So first-insurance surcharge applies to "each item even on follow-up contracts" — but it's based on the item being new to the customer. Since every item is new, it always applies.

> The first insurance surcharge still applies to the new sword, even though the customer is on a follow-up contract — each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer history.

So the surcharge applies to EVERY item in EVERY quote, always. This means my test #2 "single sword quote yields 105 G" is WRONG based on the spec — it should be 115 (100 + 10 first insurance + 5 fee).

Confusing. Let me look at "Modifier scope on multi-item policies":
> item-specific modifiers (cursed, high enchantment) apply to the base premium of the affected item; policy-wide modifiers (loyalty, first insurance, follow-up contract) apply to the policy base premium (the sum of all item base premiums); the processing fee is added at the very end

So first-insurance is POLICY-WIDE, applied to the POLICY BASE PREMIUM (sum of all item bases, with block discounts applied to the base sum). 

So for the newcomer cursed sword:
- policy base premium = 100 (sword base)
- cursed surcharge (item-specific) = 50 (50% of sword base)
- first insurance (policy-wide) = 10 (10% of 100 policy base premium)

For single newcomer sword: 100 base + 10 first insurance + 5 fee = 115.

So my test list is WRONG. I need to redo it. But wait — let me reconsider: I have 11 passing tests, all of which have wrong expected values. I need to fix them.

So the spec examples like "Sword: 1000 G insurance value, 100 G base premium" are about BASE premiums, not final quote premiums. The clarifying spec section says these are BASE values before integration with modifiers.

But the CLI input/output spec says quote returns final `premium` (integer). So when I quote a single sword, I should get the FINAL premium WITH all applicable modifiers.

I need to fix this carefully. Let me re-examine the spec ONE more time to be sure.

The spec section "Premium modifiers" lists 6 rules. Each is a rule that may or may not apply. The clarifying example "newcomer with a cursed sword" applies: base 100 + curse 50 + first-insurance 10 = 160 + 5 fee = 165.

For a newcomer with a non-cursed sword (no curse), what's the premium? Following the same rules: 100 base + first-insurance 10 + 5 fee = 115.

But wait — when does first-insurance NOT apply? Reading: "A first insurance carries a 10% initial assessment surcharge." This is the rule. So it applies to FIRST insurances. What's a "first insurance"? The clarifying question says "each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer history". So... every item is a first insurance, always.

Then ALL quotes have +10% first-insurance. So:
- 1 sword = 100 + 10 + 5 = 115 (NOT 105)
- empty = 5 (no items, so 0 + 0 + 5 = 5)

My test values were wrong. Let me update them all. I'll rebuild the test list with correct values and re-run all tests.

But wait — this introduces a difficulty: with all 10 previous tests' expected values wrong, fixing them will make all of them fail until I implement first-insurance. Let me update them all and then re-enter the green phase.

Actually — strict TDD says: one test at a time. The cleanest path: keep current 11 tests passing (they may have wrong descriptions but they're characterizing current behavior with incomplete spec compliance). When I add new tests that exercise first-insurance properly, those will force the implementation to evolve. But then my older tests will BREAK because the implementation will start adding first-insurance.

So I HAVE to update the older tests. The cleanest sequence:
1. Update test values for tests 1-11 to match the correct spec (with first-insurance applied to all items always).
2. Run tests — they will fail.
3. Add first-insurance to implementation to make them pass.
4. Continue with cursed-sword test.

Actually no — let me re-examine the spec once more. The spec says:
- "A first insurance carries a 10% initial assessment surcharge"

Maybe my interpretation is wrong. Maybe "a first insurance" means literally "a customer's first insurance contract" — i.e., it applies only when this is the customer's first contract. The clarifying question clarifies that for follow-up customers, each item still gets the surcharge.

Hmm. The clarification "each item in a quote is treated as a first insurance, regardless of customer history" — this could be read as:
- Interpretation A: Every quote (every item) always gets the +10% surcharge regardless of customer history.
- Interpretation B: When a customer is on a follow-up contract, the new items are still considered "first insurance" for those items (so the 10% applies per item, regardless of customer's overall contract history).

Both interpretations lead to the same result for the integration examples (since each integration example has new items). But for previous quotes with prior items... the spec doesn't cover that.

Let me check both integration examples:
- Newcomer (0 years, no prev contract), cursed sword → 165. 100 + 50 + 10 (first-ins) + 5 = 165. ✓ Confirms +10 G applied.
- Long-standing (3 years), 2nd contract, cursed sword ench 7 → 160. Math: 100 + 50 + 30 - 20 + 10 - 15 + 5 = 160. The +10 first-insurance applies even though this is the 2nd contract.

So the answer to the question is: the +10% first-insurance surcharge applies to every item in every quote, always. The follow-up contract discount (-15%) is separate and applies to follow-up contracts.

OK so my original interpretation is correct. Test values 1-11 need updating.
